_comment: Agni Sūktam — a contemporary musical setting weaving canonical Vedic verses to Agni with modern English poetic interludes. Source identification: opening + closing 'agnaye svāhā' are standard ritual offerings; lines 1-3 are RV 1.1.1-3 (the very first verses of the Rigveda); line 4 is a composite Agni-Mitra-Varuṇa invocation; line 6 is RV 1.189.1 / Īśā Up. 18 (the famous 'agne naya supathā' closing prayer); line 7 is RV 2.23.1 (gaṇānāṃ tvā gaṇapatiṃ); line 8 is a yajña-invocation of Agni as banner of the rite; line 9 is RV 1.1.9 (sa naḥ piteva sūnave). The contemporary English verses (Flare in the marrow / O Agni blaze through my shadows / Strike the cosmic forge / Fan the ashes…) are deliberately NOT included as lyrics_lines — they're modern poetic overlay, not Sanskrit content. The lyric panel highlights only Sanskrit; the audio plays everything. Timings are ESTIMATES; adjust after watching the actual video to match the audio.
title: Agni Sūktam (contemporary setting of Rigveda 1.1 + Īśā Up. 18)

lines.devanagari: अग्नये स्वाहा । अग्नये इदं न मम ॥
lines.iast: agnaye svāhā | agnaye idaṃ na mama ||
lines.english_literal: To Agni, svāhā! To Agni this — not mine.
lines.english_poetic: To Agni, the offering! This is for Agni — it is not for me.
lines.explanation: The standard offering formula of every Vedic yajña. 'Svāhā' is the invocation cast as the ghee meets the flame; 'idaṃ na mama' ('this is not mine') is the act of renunciation — the offerer releases ownership of the gift at the moment it is given. The ritual essence of all sacrifice in three words.

words.meaning: the offering-utterance; let it be well-given

lines.devanagari: अग्निमीळे पुरोहितं यज्ञस्य देवमृत्विजम् । होतारं रत्नधातमम् ॥
lines.iast: agnim īḷe purohitaṃ yajñasya devam ṛtvijam | hotāraṃ ratnadhātamam ||
lines.english_literal: Agni I praise — the household-priest of the sacrifice, god, ritual-minister, invoker, the most-bestowing-of-riches.
lines.english_poetic: I praise Agni — household priest of the sacrifice, the god, the seasonal-officiant, the invoker, lavisher of treasures.
lines.explanation: Rigveda 1.1.1 — the opening verse of the entire Veda. Hindu tradition holds that the recorded knowledge of humanity begins with this praise of Agni. Each epithet names a specific ritual role: purohita (the priest installed in the household), ṛtvij (the priest of the season's rite), hotṛ (the invoker who calls the gods to the offering), and ratnadhātama ('bestower of treasures' in the superlative).

words.meaning: household priest, one placed in front
seasonal priest, officiant of the rite

lines.devanagari: अग्निः पूर्वेभिर्ऋषिभिरीड्यो नूतनैरुत । स देवाँ एह वक्षति ॥
lines.iast: agniḥ pūrvebhir ṛṣibhir īḍyo nūtanair uta | sa devān eha vakṣati ||
lines.english_literal: Agni — by ancient seers worthy-of-praise, and by new (ones) too. He will bring the gods hither.
lines.english_poetic: Agni — worthy of praise by the ancient seers and by the new ones alike. He will carry the gods here to the rite.
lines.explanation: Rigveda 1.1.2 — Agni bridges generations. Worshipped by the original ṛṣis who heard the Veda and by their living successors, he carries the rite's offerings up to the gods and the gods' presence down to the altar. The verb 'vakṣati' (will bring) sets the rite in active anticipation.

lines.devanagari: अग्निना रयिमश्नवत् पोषमेव दिवेदिवे । यशसं वीरवत्तमम् ॥
lines.iast: agninā rayim aśnavat poṣam eva dive dive | yaśasaṃ vīravattamam ||
lines.english_literal: Through Agni one obtains wealth, nourishment indeed, day by day — glorious, having the most heroes.
lines.english_poetic: Through Agni one wins wealth and sustenance, day after day — fame, and the very fullness of heroes.
lines.explanation: Rigveda 1.1.3 — the temporal cadence of Agni's blessing. 'Dive dive' ('day by day') is the daily renewal that the offering establishes; 'vīravattama' literally 'most-having-of-heroes' — the well-tended fire produces a household full of strong sons (in the Vedic frame, the marker of prosperity).

lines.devanagari: अग्निर्मुखं कव्यानां भगो देवानामुत । स नो मित्रो वरुणो मामहन्ताम् ॥
lines.iast: agnir mukhaṃ kavyānāṃ bhago devānām uta | sa no mitro varuṇo māmahantām ||
lines.english_literal: Agni — the mouth of the offerings, and Bhaga among the gods. May he, Mitra and Varuṇa, magnify us.
lines.english_poetic: Agni is the mouth that receives our offerings, the giver among the gods. May he, with Mitra and Varuṇa, magnify us with grace.
lines.explanation: A composite Vedic Agni invocation. 'Mouth of the offerings' is the classical image: every oblation poured into the fire reaches the gods through Agni's mouth — he is the sole conduit. The closing benediction invokes Mitra (the god of contracts) and Varuṇa (cosmic order), placing this rite under their joint care.

words.meaning: of the offerings (to the manes or gods)

lines.english_poetic: May it be offered! May it be offered! May it be offered!
lines.explanation: The threefold svāhā — three consecutive offerings into the fire. In yajña practice each pour is timed to the utterance; the threefold repetition often marks the completion of a major oblation sequence.

words.meaning: the offering-utterance; let it be well-given

lines.devanagari: अग्ने नय सुपथा राये अस्मान् विश्वानि देव वयुनानि विद्वान् । युयोध्यस्मज्जुहुराणमेनो भूयिष्ठां ते नमउक्तिं विधेम ॥
lines.iast: agne naya supathā rāye asmān viśvāni deva vayunāni vidvān | yuyodhy asmaj juhurāṇam eno bhūyiṣṭhāṃ te nama-uktiṃ vidhema ||
lines.english_literal: O Agni, lead us by the good path to wealth, O god knowing all the paths. Separate from us the crooked sin; may we offer to you most-abundant words of homage.
lines.english_poetic: O Agni, lead us by the good path to prosperity — you, the god who knows all the ways. Take away from us the crookedness of sin; we offer you the fullest praise we can.
lines.explanation: Rigveda 1.189.1 = Īśā Upaniṣad mantra 18. Recited at the close of every formal Vedic and Vedantic recitation. The structure is a four-fold prayer: lead us → because you know all → cleanse our error → so we may praise. The word 'juhurāṇam' (literally 'going crookedly') for sin captures the Vedic conception of error as deviation from ṛta, the straight path of cosmic order.

words.meaning: to wealth, for the sake of wealth
speech of homage, word of obeisance

lines.devanagari: अग्ने गणानां त्वा गणपतिं हवामहे । कविं कवीनामुपमश्रवस्तमम् ॥
lines.iast: agne gaṇānāṃ tvā gaṇapatiṃ havāmahe | kaviṃ kavīnām upamaśravastamam ||
lines.english_literal: O Agni — we invoke you as Gaṇapati (lord) of the hosts; poet of poets, most renowned in comparison.
lines.english_poetic: O Agni — we call upon you as lord of the hosts, poet among poets, of incomparable fame.
lines.explanation: Rigveda 2.23.1 (with opening vocative 'agne') — the famous 'gaṇānāṃ tvā gaṇapatiṃ havāmahe' verse. In the Rigveda this is addressed to Brahmaṇaspati, the lord of mantras; in later Hindu tradition the same verse came to be applied to Gaṇeśa as the invocation chanted at the start of every undertaking. 'Kaviṃ kavīnām' — the seer-poet of all seer-poets — is a high Vedic title.

lines.devanagari: यज्ञस्य केतुं प्रथमं पुरस्ताद् आ ह्वये दिविषदग्निम् आ सु । दिवेदिवे इध्यमानं वाजान् ॥
lines.iast: yajñasya ketuṃ prathamam purastād ā hvaye diviṣad-agnim ā su | dive-dive idhyamānaṃ vājān ||
lines.english_literal: I invoke first, from before, the banner of the sacrifice — Agni dwelling in heaven — well (hither). Day by day kindled (he gives) strengths.
lines.english_poetic: I summon first, from the east, the banner of the sacrifice — Agni who dwells in heaven. Kindled day by day, he brings us strength.
lines.explanation: A Vedic Agni invocation in the yajña-prayoga tradition. The fire on the altar is the visible banner ('ketu') marking that this place has become the seat of the rite; Agni in heaven is simultaneously summoned to occupy that banner. 'Diviṣad' — sitting in heaven — names the celestial form; 'idhyamāna' (being kindled) the earthly. 'Vāja' is the Vedic word for vigour, strength, the prize won in the rite.

words.meaning: from in front, from the east

lines.devanagari: स नः पितेव सूनवे अग्ने सूपायनो भव । सचस्वा नः स्वस्तये ॥
lines.iast: sa naḥ piteva sūnave agne sūpāyano bhava | sacasvā naḥ svastaye ||
lines.english_literal: He, to us — like a father to a son — O Agni, be easy to approach. Accompany us for well-being.
lines.english_poetic: Like a father to his son, O Agni, be easy for us to approach. Stay with us — for our well-being.
lines.explanation: Rigveda 1.1.9 — the closing verse of the very first hymn of the Veda. The simile 'piteva sūnave' (as a father to a son) is one of the gentlest images in the entire Rigveda; the verse imagines Agni not as cosmic banner but as patient parent. The Vedic root 'sac-' (to be a companion to) carries the request: be near us. With this prayer the Veda begins.

lines.english_poetic: To Agni — the offering! The offering! The offering!
lines.explanation: The rite closes as it began — three offerings to Agni, the threefold 'svāhā' fading into the silence after the last flame.

words.meaning: the offering-utterance; let it be well-given
